english: damp / humid
Hamburg in one word. Not 'nass' (wet), not 'trocken' (dry), but in-between.

A: Why is everything so damp here?
B: That is Hamburg! Always damp.
A: Do I need an umbrella?
B: Always. Every day.
Related to English 'damp' via Proto-Germanic. Hamburg's maritime climate means feuchte Luft (humid air) year-round. Locals just accept it.
near / close
Comparative: näher (nearer). Superlative: am nächsten (nearest).

A: Is the harbor near?
B: Yes, very near. Only ten minutes.
A: Can you walk there?
B: Yes, it is the next street.
'Nah' and 'nahe' are both used, but in different grammatical slots. 'Der Hafen ist nah' (predicative) vs. 'nahe dem Hafen' (prepositional).
near (preposition/adverb form)
Slightly more literary than 'nah.' Takes dative: 'nahe dem Hafen.'

A: The hotel is near the main train station.
B: Very practical!
A: Yes, everything is nearby.
B: Perfect for a short business trip.
'Nahe' as a preposition takes dative: 'nahe dem Hafen.' As an adverb, it works like 'nah.' Both forms trace back to the same Proto-Germanic root.
This northern German greeting works at any time of day, not just mornings.
Hamburg faces northwest, toward the sea between Germany and England.
Which word describes a defined route between two cities?
The Hamburg-Berlin route takes about two hours.
So, the route to the hotel is not far. Ten minutes, if the traffic cooperates.
Hamburg is bigger than I thought. And damper.
Master urban transport vocabulary. Learn road and traffic terms. Navigate a German city like a local.
Learn 20 transport and urban vocabulary words
Understand compound noun gender rules
Navigate Hamburg's streets and transit
the subway
Untergrund (underground) + Bahn (railway). The yellow U sign is iconic.

A: How do I get to the harbor?
B: Take the subway.
A: Which line?
B: The U3 to Landungsbrücken.
Hamburg's U-Bahn opened in 1912. Berlin, Munich, Hamburg, and Nuremberg all have U-Bahn systems. The yellow U sign marks every entrance.
the streetcar / tram
Short for 'Trambahn.' Most German cities have tram networks.

A: Does a tram run here?
B: No, Hamburg no longer has a tram.
A: Really? Why not?
B: The city removed them in 1978.
Hamburg actually removed its trams in 1978, but most German cities kept theirs. Berlin has one of Europe's largest tram networks with over 190 km of track.
the ferry
From 'fahren' (to travel). Hamburg's harbor ferries are part of public transport.

A: Can you take the ferry?
B: Yes! The ferry is part of the HVV.
A: Do I need an extra ticket?
B: No, your normal ticket is enough.
Hamburg's harbor ferries are part of the public transport system (HVV). You can ride them with a normal transit ticket, making them the cheapest harbor tour in the city.
the railway
Eisen (iron) + Bahn (track). Literally 'iron track.'

A: The railway is very important in Germany.
B: Yes, many people travel by train.
A: Since when has the railway existed?
B: Since 1835 in Germany.
The first German Eisenbahn ran in 1835 between Nürnberg and Fürth, just 6 km. Today Germany has over 33,000 km of track.
the vehicle
Fahr (drive) + Zeug (stuff/thing). Literally 'driving-thing.'

A: What kind of vehicle do you have?
B: I have a small car.
A: In Hamburg you do not need a vehicle.
B: True, the subway is enough.
The Zeug pattern is productive: Fahrzeug (vehicle), Flugzeug (airplane = flying-thing), Werkzeug (tool = work-thing), Spielzeug (toy = play-thing). Gender always comes from the last element.
the motorcycle
Motor + Rad (wheel). Germany is the birthplace of the motorcycle.

A: Do you ride a motorcycle?
B: Yes, in summer. In winter it is too cold.
A: Is it dangerous?
B: Only if you do not pay attention.
Gottlieb Daimler built the first motorcycle in 1885 in Bad Cannstatt, Germany. Today Germany has over 4 million registered motorcycles.
the motor vehicle
Kraft (power) + Fahrzeug (vehicle). Abbreviated KFZ. On every vehicle registration.

A: What is a Kraftfahrzeug?
B: Any vehicle with an engine.
A: So car, motorcycle, truck?
B: Exactly. Everything with an engine is a KFZ.
KFZ is one of Germany's most common abbreviations. 'KFZ-Versicherung' (vehicle insurance) and 'KFZ-Zulassungsstelle' (vehicle registration office) are words every German driver knows.
the roadway / carriageway
Fahr (drive) + Bahn (track). The actual surface where vehicles drive.

A: Stay on the roadway!
B: I am a cyclist. Where should I ride?
A: On the bike path, not on the roadway.
B: Oh, sorry!
In Germany, the Fahrbahn is strictly for motor vehicles. Cyclists use the Radweg (bike path) and pedestrians use the Gehsteig (sidewalk). Mixing them up is frowned upon.
the one-way street
Ein (one) + Bahn (track) + Straße (street). Common in old town centers.

A: Can I turn right here?
B: No, that is a one-way street!
A: In which direction?
B: Only to the left.
German old town centers are full of Einbahnstraßen. The blue sign with a white arrow marks them. Tourists often learn this word the hard way.
the entry / entrance (for vehicles)
Ein (in) + Fahrt (drive). On garage doors: 'Einfahrt freihalten!'

A: Where is the entrance to the parking garage?
B: The next street on the right.
A: Is the entrance wide enough?
B: Yes, no problem.
'Einfahrt freihalten!' (Keep entrance clear!) is one of the most common signs on German garage doors. Germans take this very seriously.
the exit (for vehicles)
Aus (out) + Fahrt (drive). On the Autobahn, every exit has a number.

A: Which exit do we need to take?
B: Exit 23: Hamburg-Harburg.
A: How far is that?
B: Ten more kilometers.
Every tourist giggles at how often they see 'Ausfahrt' on the Autobahn. It just means 'exit,' but it sounds exotic when you cannot read German yet.
the pedestrian (male)
Fuß (foot) + Gänger (goer). Germany has strict pedestrian rules.

A: Only pedestrians may walk here.
B: No cars?
A: No, this is a pedestrian zone.
B: Very pleasant!
Jaywalking is frowned upon in Germany. Crossing on red earns you disapproving looks, especially from parents with children. In Switzerland, the sidewalk is called 'Trottoir' (from French).
the pedestrian (female)
Feminine form of Fußgänger. The -in suffix for feminine persons.

A: The pedestrian is waiting at the traffic light.
B: Very exemplary!
A: In Hamburg everyone waits at red.
B: That is typically northern German.
The -in suffix for feminine persons is consistent: Lehrer/Lehrerin, Fahrer/Fahrerin, Fußgänger/Fußgängerin. It is one of German's most productive patterns.
the pedestrian zone
Fußgänger + Zone. No cars, only people, shops, and cafes.

A: Is there a pedestrian zone in Hamburg?
B: Yes, Mönckebergstraße.
A: Can you shop there?
B: Yes, many shops and department stores.
Every German city center has a Fußgängerzone. Hamburg's Mönckebergstraße is one of the city's main shopping streets.
the cyclist (male)
Rad (wheel/bicycle) + Fahrer (driver). Germany has excellent cycling infrastructure.

A: Watch out, a cyclist!
B: Where? I do not see him.
A: On the bike path to the left.
B: Oh yes, thanks!
Germany has excellent cycling infrastructure. In Switzerland, a bicycle is called 'Velo' (from French vélocipède), not 'Fahrrad.'
the cyclist (female)
Feminine cyclist. Same -in pattern.

A: The cyclist is riding very fast.
B: Yes, many cyclists are out and about in Hamburg.
A: Are there good bike paths?
B: Yes, the city invests a lot in bike paths.
Hamburg is investing heavily in cycling infrastructure with the 'Velorouten' network, connecting the city's neighborhoods with dedicated bike highways.
the zone
From Greek 'zone' (belt/girdle). Very versatile word in German.

A: We are traveling in Zone A.
B: Do we need a different ticket for Zone B?
A: Yes, an extension ticket.
B: That is complicated!
'Umweltzone' (low-emission zone) covers most German city centers. Only vehicles with a green sticker may enter. 'Zeitzone' = time zone. A versatile word.
the lane / track / trace
Also means 'trace' or 'clue' in detective stories.

A: Stay in the right lane.
B: Why?
A: The left lane is only for passing.
B: Ah, like on the Autobahn.
On the Autobahn, 'die rechte Spur' (right lane) is for normal driving, 'die linke Spur' (left lane) is for overtaking only. Germans take this rule very seriously.
the curve / bend
Related to English 'curve' via Latin 'curvus.'

A: Caution, sharp curve!
B: I am driving slowly.
A: These curves are dangerous in the rain.
B: Yes, the road is slippery.
On mountain roads, you see 'Vorsicht, scharfe Kurve!' (Caution, sharp curve!) constantly. The word also works figuratively: 'die Kurve kriegen' = to get it together.
the location / situation
Context determines meaning: 'location' or 'situation.'

A: The location of the hotel is perfect.
B: Yes, right on the harbor.
A: And the political situation?
B: That is a different question!
'Die Lage des Hotels' (the location of the hotel) sneaks in a genitive construction. You will learn this case formally in the next lesson.
KFZ is a ___-vehicle. What does that first element contribute?
A zone where only pedestrians walk, no cars allowed.
In Hamburg's city center there is a large pedestrian zone.
In compound nouns, the LAST element determines the article. Fahrzeug is neuter.
Take the next exit on the right lane. Two road/highway terms.
Take the next exit on the right lane.
Navigate a formal German meeting. Learn the genitive case: 'des Mannes, der Frau.' Understand German title culture.
Master building and location vocabulary
Learn the genitive case
Navigate professional introductions
Understand German title culture
The company's office is right on the harbor. Through the windows, Verumius sees the cranes of the harbor city.
May I introduce? This is Herr Professor Doktor Schmidt, the head of the department.
Good day, Herr Professor Doktor Schmidt. It is an honor.
In Hamburg we say all titles. The colleague's name is important, but the titles are sacred!
the building
From 'bauen' (to build). The Ge- prefix creates a collective noun.

A: What kind of building is that?
B: That is the Elbphilharmonie.
A: An impressive building!
B: Yes, Hamburg's most famous building.
The Ge- prefix creates collective nouns: Gebäude (building), Gepäck (luggage), Gebirge (mountain range). The Elbphilharmonie is Hamburg's most famous Gebäude.
the tower
From Latin 'turris.' Hamburg's Michel (St. Michaelis) is the city's landmark.

A: Do you see the tower there?
B: Yes, that is the Michel.
A: How tall is it?
B: 132 meters. You can climb up.
Hamburg's Michel (St. Michaelis church tower) is the city's landmark. Berlin's Fernsehturm (TV tower) is 368 m tall. The word Turm is related to English 'tower.'
the courtyard / yard / farm
One of German's most productive compound elements.

A: The courtyard of the building is beautiful.
B: Yes, with trees and a bench.
A: Do all buildings have a courtyard?
B: Many old buildings, yes.
'Hof' appears in many compounds: Bahnhof (train yard), Bauernhof (farm), Innenhof (inner courtyard), Friedhof (peace-yard = cemetery). One root, endless compounds.
the suburb
Vor (before/in front of) + Ort (place). Literally 'fore-place.'

A: Do you live in Hamburg?
B: No, in a suburb.
A: Is it far to work?
B: No, only 20 minutes by commuter rail.
Hamburg's Vororte stretch far along the Elbe. German suburbs feel different from American ones: denser, with better public transport and local bakeries.
the bank / shore (of a river)
Related to English 'over' via Proto-Germanic.

A: Shall we walk along the bank of the Elbe?
B: Good idea! The shore is very beautiful here.
A: Can you sit there?
B: Yes, there are benches on the bank.
Hamburg's HafenCity neighborhood sits on the Ufer der Elbe. 'Am Ufer' (on the bank) is one of the most scenic phrases in Hamburg's vocabulary.
the coast
From Latin 'costa' (rib/side). Famous for Wattwanderung (mudflat hiking).

A: Have you been to the coast?
B: Yes, to the North Sea coast.
A: What is it like there?
B: Windy and beautiful!
The German North Sea coast is famous for Wattwanderung (mudflat hiking). At low tide, you can walk kilometers into the sea. UNESCO World Heritage since 2009.
the ocean
From Greek 'Okeanos.' Hamburg's connection to the Atlantic made it great.

A: The Atlantic Ocean is not far from Hamburg.
B: Well, the North Sea is closer.
A: The North Sea is part of the ocean.
B: True!
Hamburg's connection to the Atlantic via the Elbe made it one of Europe's greatest trading cities. The word 'Übersee' (overseas) is deeply woven into Hamburg's identity.
the distance (formal)
From Latin 'distantia.' More formal than 'Entfernung.'

A: The distance between Hamburg and Berlin is 290 km.
B: That is not far.
A: By ICE only 1:45.
B: Very practical.
Distanz is used in professional and scientific contexts. For everyday speech, Germans prefer Entfernung.
the distance (everyday)
From 'entfernen' (to remove/move away). More everyday than Distanz.

A: What is the distance to the harbor?
B: About two kilometers.
A: Can you walk?
B: Yes, a nice walk.
Entfernung comes from ent- (away) + fernen (from fern = far). 'In kurzer Entfernung' = at a short distance. More common in daily speech than Distanz.
urban / municipal
From 'Stadt' (city). The adjective form of Stadt.

A: Are there municipal buses?
B: Yes, the municipal public transport is good.
A: What is the company called?
B: HVV, the Hamburg Transport Association.
Hamburg's städtische character differs sharply from rural Schleswig-Holstein just north of the city. The urban-rural contrast is one of Germany's defining features.
regional
Same as English. Pronounced 'ray-gee-oh-NAHL.'

A: Are there regional differences?
B: Yes, many! Language, food, culture.
A: For example?
B: In Hamburg they say 'Moin', in Bavaria 'Grüß Gott.'
Germany's regional diversity is one of its defining features. From greetings to bread rolls to humor: every Region has its own flavor.
the passenger (male)
From French 'passager.' Used for ship, plane, and train passengers.

A: How many passengers does the ship have?
B: Over 3,000 passengers.
A: That is an enormous ship!
B: Yes, a cruise ship.
In Switzerland, the train platform is called 'Perron' (from French), not 'Bahnsteig.' Swiss German has many French loanwords.
the passenger (female)
Feminine form. The -in suffix applies to all person-nouns.

A: The passenger forgot her luggage.
B: On the train?
A: Yes, in the dining car.
B: She should call the lost and found.
The Passagier/Passagierin pair follows the same -in pattern as Fahrer/Fahrerin. Consistent and productive.
the steward (male)
Borrowed from English. Used for male flight attendants and ship stewards.

A: The steward is bringing the food.
B: On the ship?
A: Yes, on the cruise ship.
B: How nice!
Borrowed from English but pronounced with a German R. Used on ships and planes. Increasingly replaced by gender-neutral alternatives in modern German.
the stewardess / flight attendant (female)
From English. Increasingly replaced by 'Flugbegleiterin.'

A: The stewardess is explaining the safety rules.
B: What is the modern term?
A: Flugbegleiterin (flight companion).
B: That sounds more professional.
'Flugbegleiterin' (flight companion, feminine) is the modern replacement for Stewardess. The shift reflects German's ongoing effort to modernize gendered job titles.
the truck
Short for 'Lastkraftwagen' (LKW). Everyday word for truck.

A: A large truck is blocking the street.
B: That happens often in Hamburg.
A: Because of the harbor?
B: Yes, many trucks drive to the harbor.
The formal term is LKW (Lastkraftwagen), but everyone says Laster in everyday speech. On the Autobahn, Laster have a speed limit of 80 km/h.
the transport
From Latin 'transportare.' Hamburg handles 130 million tons of cargo annually.

A: The transport of the goods takes three days.
B: From where?
A: From Hamburg's harbor to Berlin.
B: That is fast.
Hamburg's port handles 130 million tons of cargo annually. 'Öffentlicher Nahverkehr' (public transit) is more common than 'öffentlicher Transport' in everyday German.
to transport
Regular verb with the -ieren ending from French/Latin.

A: The ships transport goods from around the world.
B: What kind of goods?
A: Everything: cars, electronics, food.
B: Hamburg's harbor is enormous.
Verbs ending in -ieren (transportieren, organisieren, telefonieren) are always regular and never get ge- in the past participle: transportiert, not getransportiert.
dense / thick / tight
'Dichter Nebel' = thick fog. Also slang for 'drunk' (informal).

A: The fog is very thick today.
B: Yes, you can barely see anything.
A: Typical Hamburg!
B: Thick fog, damp air.
'Dicht besiedelt' (densely populated) describes Hamburg's inner city. The word also means 'tight' (a container is dicht) and, in slang, 'drunk.'
the tourism
From French/English. The -ismus suffix marks abstract concepts, always masculine.

A: Tourism is important for Hamburg.
B: How many tourists come per year?
A: Over 15 million overnight stays.
B: Impressive!
The -ismus suffix is always masculine: Tourismus, Kapitalismus, Optimismus. It marks abstract concepts borrowed through Latin/French/English.

In English, you say 'the man's book' or 'the book of the man.' German uses the genitive case.

Masculine/neuter: change the article to 'des' and add -(e)s to the noun.
Feminine/plural: change the article to 'der,' noun stays the same.

Maskulin: der Mann becomes des Mannes (of the man)
Feminin: die Frau becomes der Frau (of the woman)
Neutral: das Kind becomes des Kindes (of the child)
Plural: die Kinder becomes der Kinder (of the children)

Examples from the story:
'der Leiter der Abteilung' = the head of the department
'der Name des Kollegen' = the colleague's name
'die Atmosphäre des Hafens' = the atmosphere of the harbor

In casual speech, many Germans avoid genitive and use 'von + dative' instead:
'das Buch von dem Mann' instead of 'das Buch des Mannes.'
But in writing, professional contexts, and with prepositions like 'während,' genitive is essential. Northern Germany (Hamburg!) uses genitive more than southern Germany.

One-syllable nouns get -es: des Mannes, des Kindes.
Multi-syllable nouns get just -s: des Hafens, des Gebäudes.
Masculine and neuter: des/eines + noun + -(e)s. Feminine and plural: der + noun unchanged.

Two ways to express possession:

1. Name + s: Peters Auto = Peter's car. NO apostrophe!
2. Article genitive: das Auto des Mannes.

Never mix: Des Peters Auto (wrong). Peters Auto (right).

The 'Deppenapostroph' (idiot apostrophe): Peter's Auto is grammatically wrong.
German genitive-s comes WITHOUT an apostrophe.

Exception: names ending in -s, -x, -z use an apostrophe:
Max' Auto, Hans' Buch (or: das Auto von Max)

This also applies to place names: Berlins Museen, Hamburgs Hafen.
Abteilung is feminine. Feminine genitive changes 'die' to 'der.' The head OF the department.
Hafen is masculine. Masculine genitive: des + noun + -(e)s.
Kollege is masculine. Which genitive article goes with masculine nouns?
The colleague's name is Schmidt.
Herr Professor Doktor Schmidt. Am I supposed to say that every time? Every time, all the titles?
The first time: yes. After that, Herr Schmidt is enough. The rule is simple: better too polite than too casual.
Master genitive case formation. Learn N-Deklination nouns (der Herr, der Kollege, der Junge). Understand German traffic and driving vocabulary.
Practice genitive case formation
Learn N-Deklination noun patterns
Master traffic and driving vocabulary
Combine genitive with N-Deklination
the object / item
Gegen (against) + Stand (standing). Something you encounter.

A: Do you have personal belongings with you?
B: Yes, my laptop and my phone.
A: Please do not leave any items on the train.
B: Of course.
'Persönliche Gegenstände' (personal belongings) is an announcement you hear on every German train. The word literally means 'against-standing' thing.
the taste / flavor
From 'schmecken' (to taste). Also means aesthetic sense.

A: How is the taste?
B: Very good! Salty and fresh.
A: Hamburg has good taste in food.
B: Especially with fish!
'Guter Geschmack' means both good taste in food AND good aesthetic sense. 'Geschmacklos' (tasteless) works the same way: bad-tasting or tacky.
the speed
From 'geschwind' (swift). A famously long word at 17 letters.

A: How fast is the ICE?
B: Up to 300 km/h.
A: That is fast!
B: Yes, the fastest connection.
On the Autobahn, sections without speed limit are marked 'Keine Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung.' This word alone is 30 characters of German compound mastery.
the speed limit
Geschwindigkeit + Beschränkung (restriction). 30 characters long.

A: Is there a speed limit here?
B: Yes, 50 km/h.
A: And on the Autobahn?
B: Sometimes none!
This is the word that makes German famous for long compounds. Posted as road signs: '50' in a red circle. Most Germans just say 'Tempolimit' in conversation.
the reservation
From 'reservieren.' The -ung suffix is always feminine.

A: Do you have a reservation?
B: Yes, under the name Verumius.
A: For two people?
B: Yes, exactly.
'Haben Sie eine Reservierung?' is what every hotel and restaurant asks. For trains, you pay extra for a 'Sitzplatzreservierung' (seat reservation).
the return trip
Rück (back) + Fahrt (journey). 'Hin- und Rückfahrt' = round trip.

A: When is the return trip?
B: Tomorrow evening.
A: Do you have a ticket for the return?
B: Yes, a round trip.
On train tickets, 'einfach' means one way and 'hin und zurück' means round trip. The Rückfahrt is always the homeward leg.
the tour / sightseeing trip
Rund (round) + Fahrt (journey). Hamburg's Hafenrundfahrt is iconic.

A: Shall we do a tour?
B: Yes, a harbor tour!
A: How long does the tour take?
B: About one hour.
Hamburg's Hafenrundfahrt (harbor tour) is the city's most popular tourist activity. Small boats navigate between enormous container ships.
the detour
Um (around) + Leitung (leading). Yellow signs guide you around construction.

A: The road is closed!
B: There is a detour.
A: Where does the detour lead?
B: Follow the yellow signs.
On German roads, the yellow 'Umleitung' signs guide you around construction. Germans trust these signs completely, even when the detour seems absurdly long.
the exchange / return (of goods)
Um (exchange) + Tausch (swap). Standard retail policy.

A: I would like to make an exchange.
B: Do you have the receipt?
A: Yes, here.
B: No problem. What would you like to exchange?
'Umtausch nur mit Kassenbon' (exchange only with receipt) is the standard policy in German retail. No receipt, no exchange, no exceptions.
the right of way
Vor (before) + Fahrt (driving). The yellow diamond sign means you have it.

A: Who has right of way here?
B: The sign says: Yield!
A: So I have to wait?
B: Yes, the other driver has right of way.
'Vorfahrt beachten!' (Yield!) is one of the most important road signs. The yellow diamond sign means you have Vorfahrt. Critical for driving in Germany.
the stop
From 'halten' (to stop/hold). On buses and trains: 'Nächster Halt...'

A: Next stop: Jungfernstieg.
B: That is our station!
A: Quick, we have to get off.
B: I am coming!
'Nächster Halt' (next stop) is the phrase you hear most on German public transport. Also: 'Halt! Stehenbleiben!' in action movies.
the traffic jam
Germany's Autobahn: famous for no speed limit AND epic traffic jams.

A: There is a long traffic jam on the A7.
B: How long do we have to wait?
A: Maybe an hour.
B: Then let us take the detour.
'Im Stau stehen' (to be stuck in a traffic jam) is a German national experience. The ADAC (German automobile club) reports Stau updates constantly, especially on holiday weekends.
the gas station
Tank + Stelle (place). Also convenience stores. Open late.

A: We need to refuel.
B: Where is the nearest gas station?
A: In two kilometers.
B: Good, we still have enough gasoline.
German Tankstellen are also convenience stores. After 8 PM, they are often the only shops open. 'Tanken' = to refuel. 'Volltanken' = fill it up.
the breakdown
Also used metaphorically: 'Das war eine Panne' = That was a blunder.

A: We have a breakdown!
B: What happened?
A: The tire is flat.
B: Call the ADAC!
'Eine Panne haben' works for both car breakdowns and embarrassing blunders. From French 'panne.' The ADAC is Germany's roadside assistance, like AAA.
the speed / pace
From Italian 'tempo.' 'Tempo 30' means a 30 km/h zone.

A: This is a 30 km/h zone.
B: Why so slow?
A: This is a residential area.
B: I see, because of the children.
'Tempo' is also a famous German tissue brand. 'Tempo' is to tissues what Kleenex is in the US. Asking for a 'Tempo' in Germany means asking for a tissue.
the salon
From French 'salon.' 'Friseursalon' = hair salon.

A: Where is the hair salon?
B: In the next street.
A: Do I need an appointment?
B: No, just go there.
The word retained its French pronunciation in German. 'Friseursalon' (hair salon), 'Kosmetiksalon' (beauty salon), 'Nagelsalon' (nail salon). All from French.
to hurry
'Eilt!' on documents means 'Urgent!' Related to English 'zeal.'

A: We have to hurry!
B: Why?
A: The meeting starts in five minutes.
B: Then let's go!
'Es eilt' (it is urgent) appears on official documents. Related to English 'zeal' via Proto-Germanic. 'Eilzustellung' = express delivery.
to stop
From English 'stop.' Regular verb. Also used in sports timing.

A: Stop the car!
B: Why? What happened?
A: A red light!
A: Oh, thanks! I did not see it.
'Die Zeit stoppen' means to time something with a stopwatch. 'Stopp!' as an interjection means 'Hold on!' or 'Wait!'

Some masculine nouns add -(e)n in EVERY case except nominative. These are N-Deklination nouns.

Common N-Deklination nouns:
der Herr, der Kollege, der Junge, der Kunde, der Name, der Student, der Patient, der Nachbar

Nominativ: der Herr / der Kollege
Akkusativ: den Herrn / den Kollegen
Dativ: dem Herrn / dem Kollegen
Genitiv: des Herrn / des Kollegen

How to spot them:
- Masculine nouns ending in -e (Kollege, Junge, Kunde)
- Endings: -ent (Student, Patient), -ant (Praktikant), -ist (Tourist)
- The word Herr

From the story: 'dem Herrn Kollegen' uses BOTH N-Deklination nouns in dative!

N-Deklination is a remnant of Old German weak noun declension. These nouns were originally 'weak' masculine nouns that took -n in all oblique cases. Over centuries, most nouns lost this pattern, but about 200 common ones kept it.\n\nThe key rule: if you are not sure whether a masculine noun is N-Deklination, check if it ends in -e. Most masculine nouns ending in -e (der Junge, der Kollege, der Kunde, der Nachbar) are N-Deklination.
N-Deklination nouns add -(e)n in every case except nominative. Herr adds just -n, Kollege adds -n.
Hafen is masculine. Masculine genitive uses 'des' and adds -(e)s to the noun.
Fähre is feminine. Feminine genitive changes 'die' to 'der.' The speed OF the ferry.
Kollege is an N-Deklination noun. In accusative, it adds -n.
I am introducing the colleague to you.
Herr is N-Deklination. It adds -n in every case except nominative.
Kollege is masculine AND N-Deklination. Genitive masculine article + N-Dekl ending.
The colleague's title is very long.
What forms when too many cars are on the road at once?
Master food and restaurant vocabulary. Practice genitive case in real contexts. Discover regional food differences across Germany.
Learn 20 food and restaurant vocabulary words
Practice genitive case in food contexts
Discover regional food differences
Order food in a German restaurant
After the meeting you absolutely must try a fish sandwich. That is a Hamburg obligation!
Fish sandwiches? That sounds good. I am hungry because of the long meeting.
the dish / food
Formal and slightly old-fashioned. 'Speisekarte' = menu card.

A: Which dishes do you recommend?
B: The fish dishes are excellent.
A: Do you also have vegetarian dishes?
B: Yes, on page two of the menu.
'Speisekarte' (menu card), 'Speisesaal' (dining hall), 'Speisewagen' (dining car). The word Speise appears everywhere in formal dining contexts.
the dining car
Speise + Wagen (wagon/car). Hot coffee and Currywurst at 250 km/h.

A: Is there a dining car on the train?
B: Yes, in car 7.
A: Can you have breakfast there?
B: Yes, coffee and rolls.
ICE trains have a 'Bordrestaurant' (on-board restaurant), but older trains called it Speisewagen. Hot coffee at 250 km/h is one of Germany's small luxuries.
the appetizer / starter
Vor (before) + Speise (dish). The dish before the main dish.

A: Would you like an appetizer?
B: Yes, the soup please.
A: Good choice. And for the main course?
B: The roast.
German restaurant menus list in order: Vorspeise (starter), Hauptgericht (main course), Nachspeise (dessert). The Vor/Nach pattern makes it logical.
the dessert
Nach (after) + Speise (dish). The after-dish.

A: What do you have for dessert?
B: Rote Grütze with cream.
A: What is Rote Grütze?
B: A northern German dessert with red berries.
German compound logic: before = Vor-, after = Nach-. Vorspeise/Nachspeise, Vorname/Nachname, Vormittag/Nachmittag. Crystal clear pattern.
the dessert (French loan)
From French 'desservir' (to clear the table). More cosmopolitan than Nachspeise.

A: For dessert I will have the ice cream.
B: Chocolate or vanilla?
A: Chocolate, please.
B: Excellent choice!
On upscale Hamburg menus, you see 'Dessert.' In a traditional Kneipe, it is 'Nachspeise.' The word choice signals the restaurant's formality level.
the set meal
In German, Menü means a SET MEAL, NOT the card listing dishes. The card is 'Speisekarte.'

A: We will take the set meal.
B: The daily menu with three courses?
A: Yes, appetizer, main course and dessert.
B: Perfect. Coming right up.
Das Menü means a set meal (starter + main + dessert), NOT the card listing dishes. The card is 'Speisekarte.' This false friend catches many English speakers.
the roast
Every region has its signature Braten: Schweinebraten, Sauerbraten.

A: I will have the roast.
B: Pork roast or beef roast?
A: Pork roast, please.
B: With vegetables?
'Schweinebraten' (pork roast) is Bavaria's national dish. 'Sauerbraten' (marinated pot roast) comes from the Rhineland. Every region has its signature Braten.
the ground meat
Hack (chopped) + Fleisch (meat). In northern Germany also called 'Mett.'

A: I need 500 grams of ground meat.
B: Beef or mixed?
A: Mixed, please.
B: Are you making meatballs?
In northern Germany (Hamburg!), raw ground pork on bread is called 'Mettbrötchen.' In Austria, ground meat is called 'Faschiertes.' Same product, different countries.
the chicken
Diminutive of 'Huhn.' The -chen suffix makes it neuter AND small/cute.

A: Half a chicken, please.
B: With fries?
A: Yes, and salad on the side.
B: Coming right up!
The -chen suffix always makes nouns neuter: das Mädchen (the girl), das Hühnchen (the chicken), das Brötchen (the bread roll). Every German supermarket rotisserie has Brathähnchen.
the spice
From 'Würze' (seasoning). Plural: Gewürze.

A: Which spices do you like?
B: Pepper and paprika.
A: Hamburg was a spice trading city.
B: True, because of the harbor.
Hamburg's Speicherstadt (warehouse district) stored spices from around the world for centuries. The Gewürzmuseum (spice museum) is still there today.
the vinegar
From Latin 'acetum.' Germans love Essig on salad.

A: Vinegar and oil for the salad?
B: Yes, please. More vinegar than oil.
A: You like it sour?
B: Yes, very!
'Essig und Öl' (vinegar and oil) is the standard German salad dressing. Also: 'Es ist Essig!' (slang: It is over/ruined!). A versatile word.
the honey
From Proto-Germanic '*hunagaz.' Related to English 'honey.'

A: Would you like honey on your bread?
B: Yes, gladly! Is that local honey?
A: Yes, from the beekeeper next door.
B: Delicious!
German breakfast often includes Honig on bread. 'Honigkuchen' (honey cake) is a Christmas specialty. Germany is Europe's largest honey consumer.
the sauce
From French 'sauce.' Written with ß in German (no c).

A: The sauce is excellent!
B: Thanks! That is our house specialty.
A: What is in it?
B: That is a secret!
'Bratensoße' (gravy) is essential with every Braten. Hamburg's fish restaurants are famous for their Dillsoße (dill sauce). The word comes from French.
the carrot
From French 'carotte.' In northern Germany: 'Möhre.'

A: Karotten or Möhren?
B: That is the same thing!
A: Why two words?
B: Regional: Karotte in the south, Möhre in the north.
Three words for one vegetable: Karotte (south), Möhre (north/Hamburg), gelbe Rübe (some regions). Germany's regional vocabulary in a nutshell.
the lemonade / soft drink
Short: Limo. In Germany, 'Limo' means ANY sweet carbonated drink.

A: A soft drink, please!
B: Which one? Lemon or orange?
A: Lemon.
B: Coming right up.
In Germany, 'Limo' means ANY sweet carbonated drink, not just lemon-flavored. Ordering 'eine Limo' at any Kneipe gets you a fizzy soft drink.
the snack bar / quick bite
Germany's fast food institution. Currywurst, Döner, Fischbrötchen.

A: I am hungry. Is there a snack bar here?
B: Yes, on the corner.
A: What do they have?
B: Fish sandwiches! Typical Hamburg.
The Imbiss is Germany's fast food institution. Every corner has one. In Austria, a quick snack is called 'Jause.' In Hamburg, the star Imbiss food is the Fischbrötchen.
the pub / bar
Informal, cozy, beer-focused. Hamburg's St. Pauli has legendary ones.

A: Shall we go to a pub?
B: Yes, I know a good one in St. Pauli.
A: What do we drink?
B: An Astra, the Hamburg beer!
Hamburg's St. Pauli district has legendary Kneipen. 'Kneipe' suggests informal, cozy, beer-focused. Fancier places are called 'Bar' or 'Lounge.'
The highest food compliment in casual German. From 'lecken' (to lick).

A: How does the fish taste?
B: Very delicious!
A: And the sauce?
B: Also delicious! Everything is delicious here.
'Das ist lecker!' is the universal German food compliment. Something so good you want to lick the plate. From 'lecken' (to lick).
From 'Salz' (salt). The -ig suffix turns nouns into adjectives.

A: The soup is too salty.
B: Really? I think it is good.
A: Hamburg and salt go together.
B: True, because of the sea!
Hamburg's proximity to the sea means salty air and salty food. The -ig suffix turns nouns into adjectives: Salz/salzig, Hunger/hungrig, Durst/durstig.
mild
Same as English. One of the easiest German adjectives for English speakers.

A: The cheese is mild.
B: Do you also have sharp cheese?
A: Yes, the Tilsiter.
B: I will take that!
'Milder Käse' (mild cheese), 'mildes Wetter' (mild weather), 'milde Strafe' (lenient punishment). One of the easiest German words for English speakers.
Des Bratens uses des + noun + -s. Which case describes possession or 'of the roast'?
Vorspeise is feminine. Genitive feminine changes die to what?
The sauce of the appetizer was delicious.
Northern German dialect word for carrots, mentioned in the teach card.
Während takes genitive. Essen is neuter: des + -(e)ns.
During the meal we order the dessert.
The fish sandwich was delicious. And the colleague says Möhre instead of Karotte. Every region has its own language.
Experience Hamburg's most stunning building. Master genitive prepositions: während, wegen, trotz, statt, innerhalb. Practice genitive case in flowing German sentences.
Learn genitive prepositions
Practice genitive in natural contexts
Master building and hospitality vocabulary
Experience the Elbphilharmonie through story
The Elbphilharmonie at blue hour. Verumius stands on the plaza and looks at the glass catching the last rays of daylight.
Despite the rain, the day was a success. During the meeting he spoke professional German. Because of the trip he feels stronger.
Within one day I have experienced so much. Instead of fear I now feel pride.
the bar
From English/French 'bar.' Rooftop bars with harbor views are popular in Hamburg.

A: Shall we go to the bar?
B: Yes, the rooftop bar!
A: You can see the whole harbor.
B: At night it is especially beautiful.
Hamburg's HafenCity has rooftop bars with harbor views. 'An der Bar sitzen' (to sit at the bar) is a common evening activity.
the buffet
From French 'buffet.' 'Frühstücksbuffet' = breakfast buffet.

A: The breakfast buffet is great!
B: Yes, rolls, cheese, cold cuts, muesli.
A: And eggs!
B: Typical German breakfast.
German hotels are famous for their elaborate breakfast buffets. Also spelled 'Büfett' in older German. Both spellings are correct.
the jam
EU regulation: only citrus jam can be 'Marmelade.' Everything else is 'Konfitüre.'

A: Would you like jam?
B: Yes, strawberry jam, please.
A: Technically that is Konfitüre.
B: Does not matter, tastes good!

EU regulation says only citrus preserves can be called 'Marmelade.' Everything else is technically 'Konfitüre.' But every German still says Marmelade. In Switzerland, 'Konfitüre' is the standard word.
the margarine
From French. Pronounced 'mar-ga-REE-ne' with stress on the third syllable.

A: Butter or margarine?
B: Margarine, please.
A: Healthy!
B: Well, matter of taste.
Invented as a cheap butter substitute. Pronounced 'mar-ga-REE-ne' in German, with stress on the third syllable. A breakfast-table staple alongside Butter.
the wool
Same root as English 'wool.' Essential in Hamburg's maritime climate.

A: This jacket is made of wool.
B: Good for Hamburg!
A: Yes, because of the cold.
B: Wool keeps you warm and dry.
Hamburg's maritime climate means warm Wolle clothing is essential. 'Merinowolle' (merino wool) is a favorite. 'In Wolle gepackt' means bundled up.
the leather
Related to English 'leather.' Lederhosen are Bavarian, not Hamburg.

A: A leather jacket for Hamburg?
B: Not ideal in rain.
A: True. Better a rain jacket.
B: Hamburg is not a Lederhosen city!
Lederhosen (leather pants) are Bavarian tradition, not Hamburg. Hamburg prefers practical rain jackets. 'Echtes Leder' (genuine leather) is a quality marker.
the catalog
From Greek 'katalogos' (list). Once the backbone of German mail-order shopping.

A: Do you have a catalog?
B: No, today everything is online.
A: In the past there were thick catalogs.
B: Yes, the Otto catalog was legendary.
The Otto catalog was once Germany's shopping bible. Hamburg-based Otto is still one of the world's largest online retailers. The physical Katalog era ended around 2010.
the hut / cabin
Related to English 'hut.' 'Berghütte' = mountain cabin, 'Skihütte' = ski lodge.

A: In the Alps there are many cabins.
B: Can you stay overnight?
A: Yes, and eat! Cottage cheese also comes from there.
B: Really? Interesting!
In the Alps, Hütten offer food and shelter to hikers. You can sleep in a Berghütte after a long hike. 'Hüttenkäse' (cottage cheese) literally means cabin cheese.
the castle / fortress
Hamburg = Ham (meadow/bend) + Burg (fortress). Germany has over 25,000.

A: Do you know where 'Hamburg' comes from?
B: Ham + Burg. A fortress on the Alster!
A: Exactly! The Hammaburg.
B: Unfortunately it no longer exists.
'Hamburg' literally contains this word: Ham (meadow/bend) + Burg (fortress). Germany has over 25,000 Burgen and Schlösser. Many are now hotels and museums.
the indoor pool
Halle (hall) + Bad (bath/pool). Almost every German town has one.

A: Is there an indoor pool here?
B: Yes, the municipal indoor pool.
A: Can you swim laps?
B: Yes, 50-meter pool!
Germans love swimming. Almost every town has a Hallenbad (indoor pool) and a Freibad (outdoor pool for summer). Swimming lessons for children are nearly universal.
the inn / guesthouse
Gast (guest) + Haus (house). Traditional German dining: hearty food, local beer.

A: Shall we eat at the inn?
B: Yes, they have home cooking there.
A: What is that?
B: Traditional, hearty food.
A Gasthaus is more rustic than a Restaurant: hearty food, local beer, wooden furniture. Many German villages have a Gasthaus as their social center.
the restaurant / eatery
Gast + Stätte (place). The official legal term for food-serving establishments.

A: Is that a restaurant or a Gaststätte?
B: Officially everything is a Gaststätte.
A: Even McDonald's?
B: Yes, legally speaking!
On tax forms and business licenses, every restaurant is officially a 'Gaststätte.' The word is bureaucratic German at its finest.
the coffee house
Kaffee + Haus. Vienna made the Kaffeehaus world-famous.

A: Shall we go to the coffee house?
B: Hamburg has great coffee houses.
A: Hamburg and coffee?
B: Yes! Germany's first coffee house was here, 1677.
Vienna made the Kaffeehaus world-famous, but Hamburg claims Germany's first one (1677). Coffee culture runs deep in the north. Hamburg is still the center of Germany's coffee trade.
One offers accommodation as well as food, the other is primarily a restaurant.
Warmth and cold, flat and slippery, turning and braking. Navigate Hamburg with weather vocabulary, direction verbs, and genitive prepositions.
Learn weather and texture adjectives
Master direction verbs: abbiegen, bremsen
Use genitive prepositions: während, statt
the warmth
From 'warm.' The -e suffix turns adjectives into abstract nouns (always feminine).

A: The warmth of the coffee house is pleasant.
B: Yes, outside it is cold.
A: The warmth feels good.
B: Another coffee?
The -e suffix turns adjectives into feminine abstract nouns: warm/Wärme, kalt/Kälte, nähe/Nähe, größe/Größe. Always feminine. Very productive pattern.
the cold / coldness
From 'kalt.' Same pattern as Wärme. Hamburg's maritime cold is wet and windy.

A: The cold is terrible today.
B: Despite the cold we go outside?
A: Yes! The Elbphilharmonie awaits.
B: Okay, but with wool!
'Trotz der Kälte' (despite the cold) uses genitive after 'trotz.' Hamburg's maritime Kälte is wet and windy, different from Bavaria's dry mountain cold.
flat
Northern Germany is flach. The opposite of the Alps in the south.

A: Hamburg is very flat.
B: Yes, no mountains here.
A: Completely different from southern Germany.
B: But we have the sea!
'Flachland' (flatland) describes all of northern Germany. The highest point in Hamburg is just 116 meters. Related to English 'flat' via Proto-Germanic.
smooth / slippery
'Vorsicht, glatte Straße!' = Caution, slippery road! In winter: 'Glatteis.'

A: Caution, the road is slippery!
B: Because of the rain?
A: Yes, in rain everything gets slippery.
B: I am driving slowly.
In winter, 'Glatteis' (black ice) is a serious hazard on German roads. 'Glatteiswarnung' (black ice warning) is a common weather report term.
to turn (while driving/walking)
Ab (off/away) + biegen (to bend). Separable prefix verb.

A: Turn right.
B: At the next intersection?
A: Yes, then you will see the building.
B: Thanks!
Separable: 'Biegen Sie rechts ab' = Turn right. In a subordinate clause, it merges: '...wo ich abbiegen muss.' Essential for giving and following directions.
to brake
From 'Bremse' (brake). 'Plötzlich bremsen' = to brake suddenly.

A: Brake!
B: What is wrong?
A: A pedestrian on the road!
B: Good eyes! Thanks.
'Die Bremsen sind gut' (the brakes are good) is an important phrase for car inspections. 'Bremse' also means 'horsefly,' a completely different meaning with the same spelling.
during, while
As preposition: während + Genitiv (during).\nAs conjunction: während + verb-final clause (while).

A: What did you do during the meeting?
B: I took notes.
A: While I was writing, he spoke.
B: Teamwork!
Während does double duty: as a preposition with genitive (während des Essens) and as a conjunction meaning while (während ich schlief). Both are essential for B1.
instead of
Short form of anstatt. Takes Genitiv.\nStatt des Autos = instead of the car.

A: Instead of the taxi we take the subway.
B: Good idea, that saves money.
A: Instead of coffee I drink tea.
B: Also healthier!
Statt is the short form of anstatt. Both are correct. As a preposition it takes genitive. As part of statt...zu it creates infinitive clauses meaning instead of doing.
to be located, to be situated
Reflexive. Formal alternative to sein for location.\nDas Hotel befindet sich am Hafen.

A: Where is the Elbphilharmonie located?
B: It is located in the HafenCity.
A: And the main train station?
B: It is located in the center.
More formal than sein for describing locations. Das Museum befindet sich in der Altstadt sounds more official. Essential for giving directions and reading maps.

These five prepositions ALWAYS take genitive. They elevate your German from everyday to professional.

- während (during): Während des Meetings (during the meeting)
- wegen (because of): Wegen des Regens (because of the rain)
- trotz (despite): Trotz der Kälte (despite the cold)
- statt/anstatt (instead of): Statt des Taxis (instead of the taxi)
- innerhalb (within): Innerhalb eines Tages (within one day)

The genitive article depends on gender:
- Masculine and neuter nouns use des, and the noun gets an -(e)s ending.
- Feminine and plural nouns use der, with no change to the noun.

In casual speech, Germans sometimes use dative after wegen/trotz ('wegen dem Regen'), but written and professional German demands genitive.

Beyond the big five, there are more genitive prepositions:

außerhalb = outside of: Außerhalb der Stadt (outside the city)
oberhalb = above: Oberhalb des Hafens (above the harbor)
unterhalb = below: Unterhalb der Brücke (below the bridge)
aufgrund = on the basis of: Aufgrund des Wetters (due to the weather)
anhand = by means of: Anhand der Daten (based on the data)

The big five (während, wegen, trotz, statt, innerhalb) are the most common and the ones tested on exams.
Trotz always takes the same case as wegen and während. Regen is masculine, so the article is des + -(e)s.
Während takes genitive. Meeting is neuter. Neuter genitive article?
During the meeting Verumius spoke German.
He chose the subway INSTEAD OF the taxi. Which preposition means 'instead of'?
Within ONE day. Genitive preposition + indefinite genitive article for masculine/neuter.
Within one day Verumius experienced a lot.
The lights of the Elbphilharmonie reflect on the water. Verumius walks slowly down the steps, back into the city.
During a single day I have experienced more than during an entire week in Berlin. Hamburg, you are special.
Test everything from Unit 15. Prove you can use genitive case, N-Deklination, and genitive prepositions. Show mastery of travel, food, and urban vocabulary.
Test genitive case mastery
Test N-Deklination mastery
Test genitive prepositions
Assess all unit vocabulary
Last morning in Hamburg. The suitcase stands by the door. Verumius drinks the last coffee of the stay and thinks: I am ready.
the gasoline / petrol
From the chemical compound 'benzene.' 'Bleifrei' = unleaded.

A: We need gasoline.
B: Is the gas station far?
A: No, only two kilometers.
B: Premium or diesel?
Named after the chemical compound benzene, which was named after a tree resin. 'Super' and 'Diesel' are the common pump labels at German Tankstellen.
the brake
Also means 'horsefly.' Completely different meaning, same spelling.

A: The brake does not work!
B: That is dangerous!
A: We need to go to the workshop.
B: Immediately!
'Handbremse' = handbrake, 'Bremse ziehen' = pull the brake. The word also means 'horsefly,' a completely unrelated meaning with the same spelling.
the boat
Same root as English 'boat.' The famous submarine film 'Das Boot' (1981).

A: Shall we go by boat?
B: Yes, a rowboat on the Alster!
A: Can you explore the Alster by boat?
B: Yes, that is very popular in Hamburg.
The famous submarine film 'Das Boot' (1981) made this word internationally known. In Hamburg, 'Boot' usually means the small boats on the Alster lake.
the sidewalk
Geh (walk) + Steig (path). Austrian/southern German. North: 'Bürgersteig.'

A: Stay on the sidewalk!
B: In Hamburg they say Bürgersteig.
A: And in Switzerland?
B: Trottoir!
Gehsteig (Austria/south), Bürgersteig (north), Trottoir (Switzerland). Three words for the same strip of pavement. A perfect example of regional vocabulary.
the traffic sign
Verkehr (traffic) + Zeichen (sign). Germany has over 1,000 different ones.

A: What does this traffic sign mean?
B: Yield.
A: Germany has so many traffic signs!
B: Over 1,000 different ones!
Germany has over 1,000 different Verkehrszeichen. There is even a Verkehrszeichenkatalog (traffic sign catalog). For the driving test, you need to know the most important 200.
the landing
From 'landen' (to land). The -ung suffix (always feminine) turns verbs into nouns.

A: The landing was smooth.
B: Thank God!
A: Hamburg-Fuhlsbüttel is a small airport.
B: But modern.
'Notlandung' = emergency landing, 'Mondlandung' = moon landing. The -ung suffix turns verbs into feminine nouns: landen/Landung, landen/Landung. Reliable pattern.
the lost and found office
Fund (find) + Büro (office). Every German city has one.

A: I lost my umbrella!
B: Go to the lost and found.
A: Where is it?
B: In the main station.
Every German city has a Fundbüro. Left your umbrella on the train? Check the Fundbüro at the Hauptbahnhof. Germans are surprisingly good at turning in found items.
the bread roll
In Bavaria and Austria: Semmel. In northern Germany: Brötchen.

A: Two bread rolls, please.
B: In Hamburg we say Brötchen!
A: And in Berlin?
B: Schrippe! One roll, three names.
One bread roll, many names: Brötchen (north), Semmel (Bavaria/Austria), Schrippe (Berlin), Weck (southwest), Weggli (Switzerland). Germany's greatest linguistic debate.
the dumpling
Bavarian and Austrian classic. Not common in Hamburg.

A: What are dumplings?
B: Balls made of potato or bread dough.
A: Are there any in Hamburg?
B: Rarely. Dumplings are more southern German.
'Kartoffelknödel' (potato dumpling) and 'Semmelknödel' (bread dumpling) are Bavarian/Austrian classics. In Hamburg, you are more likely to find Fischbrötchen.
the cream
Northern German word. Austrian: 'Obers.' Swiss: 'Rahm.'

A: With cream?
B: Yes, please!
A: In Austria that is called Obers.
B: And in Switzerland: Rahm.
Sahne (Germany/north), Obers (Österreich/Austria), Rahm (Schweiz/Switzerland). Same product, three countries, three words.
the whipped cream
Schlag (whip/hit) + Sahne (cream). Standard German term.

A: Cake with whipped cream, please!
B: Gladly! A lot or a little?
A: A lot!
B: A true connoisseur!
On every German cafe menu next to cake: 'mit Schlagsahne.' In Austria: 'Schlagobers.' In Switzerland: 'Schlagrahm.' One product, three words.
the muesli
From Swiss German 'Mus' (puree). Now a German breakfast staple.

A: What do you have for breakfast?
B: Muesli with yogurt.
A: Homemade?
B: No, from the supermarket.
Originally 'Birchermüsli,' invented by Swiss doctor Maximilian Bircher-Brenner around 1900. Now a German breakfast staple. Swiss spelling: Müesli.
to buckle up
An (on) + schnallen (to buckle). Separable: 'Schnall dich an!'

A: Please buckle up!
B: Of course.
A: On the Autobahn it is mandatory.
B: In Germany they take that seriously.
'Bitte anschnallen!' is the phrase on every flight and Autobahn sign. Germans take seatbelts seriously. Fines for not wearing one: 30 euros.
to honk
'Hupen verboten!' = Honking forbidden! Common sign near hospitals.

A: Why are you honking?
B: The cyclist did not see me!
A: Do not honk so loudly.
B: Sorry.
Germans consider unnecessary Hupen extremely rude. 'Hupen verboten!' signs are common near hospitals. Road rage by horn is frowned upon.
to land
Regular verb. From 'Land.' Also figurative: 'Wo bist du gelandet?'

A: The airplane is landing in Hamburg.
B: When exactly?
A: In ten minutes.
B: Perfect, we are on time.
Also used figuratively: 'Wo bist du gelandet?' = Where did you end up? Uses 'sein' in perfect tense: 'Das Flugzeug ist gelandet.'
to depart / set off
Los (off/away) + fahren (to drive). Separable. 'Los!' = Go!

A: When do we set off?
B: At eight o'clock.
A: Early!
B: Yes, the route is long.
'Los!' alone means 'Go!' Germans shout it at sports events. Separable: 'Wir fahren um 8 Uhr los.' Very common in everyday speech.
to stop / stand still
Stehen (to stand) + bleiben (to stay). Separable: 'Bleib stehen!'

A: Please stand still!
B: Why?
A: The photo is not done yet.
B: Oh, sorry. I will stand still.
'Bleib stehen!' is one of the most common commands. Also used by police: 'Stehenbleiben!' Used in everyday life when someone is about to walk into danger.
to refuel
From 'Tank.' 'Volltanken' = to fill up completely.

A: We need to refuel.
B: The next gas station is in five kilometers.
A: Is the gasoline enough?
B: Just barely.
'Volltanken' = fill it up. Also slang: 'Energie tanken' = to recharge your energy. 'Sonne tanken' = to soak up the sun. Versatile verb.
to run over
Inseparable prefix. 'Eine rote Ampel überfahren' = to run a red light.

A: He ran the red light!
B: That is very dangerous.
A: And forbidden!
B: Yes, heavy fine.
Inseparable prefix: 'Er hat die Ampel überfahren' (no ge-). 'Eine rote Ampel überfahren' = to run a red light. Serious offense with heavy fines.
to overtake / pass
Inseparable prefix. 'Rechts überholen ist verboten' on the Autobahn.

A: May I overtake here?
B: No, no-passing zone!
A: And on the Autobahn?
B: Only overtake on the left!
'Rechts überholen ist verboten' (passing on the right is forbidden) is a cardinal rule of the Autobahn. Violators face heavy fines.
Des + noun ending in -s: the classic signal for this case and gender combination.
Kälte is feminine. What does die become in the case required by wegen?
Herr is N-Deklination. In genitive, it adds -n after 'des.'
The gentleman's title was 'Professor Doktor.'
He went DESPITE the bad weather. Which preposition means 'despite'?
During THE trip. Reise is feminine. Genitive feminine article?
During the trip Verumius learned a lot about Hamburg.
Stadt is feminine. Genitive feminine: die becomes what?
The tour through the city's harbor was wonderful.
Tanken means to refuel. Where do you get fuel?
First blank: which preposition means 'during'? Second blank: N-Deklination noun in dative after 'dem.'
During the meeting Verumius was introduced to the colleague.
Despite the nervousness I did everything right. The genitive, the titles, even the fish sandwich.
Reflect on the Hamburg trip. Learn direction and movement vocabulary. Discover Germany's regional vocabulary differences. Master the unit's grammar in a final cultural context.
Learn direction and movement vocabulary
Discover regional vocabulary differences
Practice all unit grammar in cultural context
Reflect on the Hamburg experience
The ICE travels through the night. Outside: dark fields, distant village lights. Verumius writes in his notebook.
Despite the rain, despite the nervousness, it was a good day. The professor's name was hard to remember, but his smile was genuine.
Moin. One word, four letters, and suddenly a foreign city feels like a home.
Berlin Central Station. On the platform: a familiar face. Lukas with a package under his arm.
Hey! How was Hamburg?
Rainy.
Hamburg, of course. Come on, let's take the S-Bahn together.
Lukas, did you know that in Hamburg you always have to say all the titles? Herr Professor Doktor.
In Berlin we just say 'Hey.' Welcome back, Verumius.
flat-rate / all-inclusive
'Pauschalreise' = package tour. From French 'forfait.'

A: Is that an all-inclusive offer?
B: Yes, hotel, flights and food included.
A: What does the package tour cost?
B: 899 euros per person.
'Pauschalreise' (package tour) is very popular in Germany. Germans are among the world's most enthusiastic tourists. 'Pauschal bezahlen' = to pay a flat rate.
front / forward
'Der vordere Teil des Zuges' = the front part of the train.

A: Are we sitting in the front section?
B: Yes, car 1.
A: That is better for short stops.
B: True, closer to the exit.
Comparative of 'vorn' (in front). 'Vorderseite' = front side, 'Vordertür' = front door, 'Vorderrad' = front wheel. Very productive compound element.
downward
Ab (away/down) + -wärts (toward). Also figurative: 'Es geht abwärts.'

A: Things are going downhill.
B: Really? Downhill?
A: No, I mean the economy.
B: I see, unfortunately.
The -wärts suffix indicates direction: abwärts (downward), aufwärts (upward), rückwärts (backward), vorwärts (forward). A useful pattern.
upward
Auf (up) + -wärts. Opposite of abwärts.

A: The career is going up!
B: Well done!
A: Thanks. Because of the Hamburg trip.
B: See, it was worth it.
'Es geht aufwärts!' (Things are looking up!) is an optimistic expression. Opposite of 'Es geht abwärts.' The -wärts direction pattern is productive.
outside
Opposite of 'innen' (inside). 'Außenseiter' = outsider.

A: From the outside the building looks small.
B: But inside it is enormous!
A: Old outside, modern inside.
B: Typical Hamburg.
Hamburg's Außenalster (Outer Alster lake) is a famous landmark. 'Außenseiter' (outsider) literally means 'outside-sitter.' Opposite: 'innen' (inside).
across / diagonally
'Quer durch die Stadt' = across the city. Related to English 'queer.'

A: We are going across Hamburg.
B: From Altona to Wandsbek?
A: Yes, across the entire city.
B: That takes an hour.
'Querstraße' = cross street. 'Quereinstieg' = lateral entry. Related to English 'queer' (originally meaning 'oblique/odd').
up (colloquial)
Short for 'herauf.' Colloquial. The prefix rauf- attaches to verbs.

A: Come up!
B: I am coming! Which floor?
A: Third.
B: Is there no elevator?
Short for 'herauf' (up here). Informal register. 'Raufgehen' = go up, 'raufklettern' = climb up. The colloquial prefix rauf- is very common in spoken German.
backward
Rück (back) + -wärts. 'Rückwärts einparken' = reverse parking.

A: Can you reverse park?
B: Of course! That is mandatory in the driving test.
A: In my country too.
B: Backward is backward, the same everywhere!
Germans must master rückwärts einparken for the driving test. 'Rückwärtsgang' = reverse gear. The -wärts suffix indicates direction.
ahead / in advance
'Im Voraus' = in advance. 'Geradeaus' = straight ahead.

A: Please pay in advance.
B: Okay, how much?
A: 25 euros.
B: Here you are.
'Im Voraus' (in advance) and 'voraus' (ahead) are essential words. 'Voraussetzung' (prerequisite) and 'voraussichtlich' (presumably) build on the same root.
along
After noun + accusative, or before noun + dative/genitive.

A: Walk along the street.
B: How far?
A: To the harbor.
B: Along the shore?
Unusual grammar: 'die Straße entlang' (after noun, accusative) or 'entlang der Straße' (before noun, genitive). Both are correct.
This preposition describes following a path or road in one direction.
Remove, flow, park, cross, turn around, exchange: seven action verbs plus German, Austrian, and Swiss vocabulary variants. The DACH region speaks one language in many dialects.
Learn seven action verbs for movement and change
Discover DACH vocabulary variants
Practice all grammar patterns from this unit
to remove / to be distant
Ent- (away) + fernen (from fern = far). Inseparable.

A: Please remove the object.
B: Which object?
A: The suitcase there.
B: Oh, that is mine! Sorry.
Also used as adjective: 'weit entfernt' (far away), 'drei Kilometer entfernt' (three kilometers away). Inseparable prefix ent- means 'away from.'
to flow
Irregular: fließt, floss, ist geflossen. Related to English 'flow.'

A: The Elbe flows through Hamburg.
B: Where does it flow?
A: Into the North Sea.
B: The river is enormous here.
Hamburg exists because the Elbe fließt to the North Sea. Irregular conjugation: fließt, floss, ist geflossen. Related to English 'flow' and 'flood.'
to park (Swiss)
Swiss German for 'parken.' The -ieren ending follows French/Latin loan patterns.

A: Where can you park here?
B: In Switzerland they say parkieren.
A: And in Germany?
B: Parken! Without -ieren.
In Germany and Austria: 'parken.' In Switzerland: 'parkieren.' The -ieren ending follows the French/Latin loan pattern common in Swiss German.
to cross
Über (over) + queren (to cross). Inseparable. More formal than 'gehen über.'

A: Please cross the street at the crosswalk.
B: Of course!
A: In Germany you wait at red.
B: Yes, I learned that!
Inseparable: 'Er überquert die Straße.' More formal than 'über die Straße gehen.' Past participle: 'überquert' (no ge-).
to turn around
Um (around) + drehen (to turn). Separable. Also reflexive: 'sich umdrehen.'

A: Turn around!
B: Why?
A: Look, the sunset!
B: Oh, beautiful!
Separable: 'Dreh dich um!' (Turn around!). Reflexive: 'sich umdrehen.' Verumius dreht sich um und sieht Lukas auf dem Bahnsteig.
to exchange (goods/money)
Um (exchange) + tauschen (to swap). Separable.

A: Can I exchange this?
B: Only with the receipt.
A: Here it is.
B: Good. What would you like instead?
'Euro in Dollar umtauschen' = to exchange euros for dollars. 'Umtausch nur innerhalb von 14 Tagen' = exchange only within 14 days. Standard German retail policy.
the stuff / things
Very versatile. 'Zeug' appears in many compounds: Fahrzeug, Werkzeug, Spielzeug.

A: What kind of stuff is that?
B: My travel stuff.
A: You have a lot of stuff with you!
B: Yes, I always pack too much.
'Zeug' is one of German's most productive compound elements: Fahrzeug (vehicle), Flugzeug (airplane), Werkzeug (tool), Spielzeug (toy), Feuerzeug (lighter). Literally 'stuff for X.'

German is spoken in Germany (D), Austria (A), and Switzerland (CH). But each country has its own vocabulary, sometimes for everyday items.

Cream: Sahne (D) / Rahm (CH) / Obers (A)
Whipped cream: Schlagsahne (D) / Schlagrahm (CH) / Schlagobers (A)
Carrot: Karotte (D-south) / Möhre (D-north) / Rübe (D-regional)
Bread roll: Brötchen (D-north) / Semmel (D-south, A) / Weggli (CH)
Jam: Marmelade (D, A) / Konfitüre (CH, formal D)
Sidewalk: Bürgersteig (D-north) / Gehsteig (D-south, A) / Trottoir (CH)
Bicycle: Fahrrad (D, A) / Velo (CH)
Parking: parken (D, A) / parkieren (CH)

Hamburg's 'Moin!' vs Berlin's 'Hallo' vs Bavaria's 'Grüß Gott' vs Switzerland's 'Grüezi' shows that even greetings split by region.

Germany was not unified until 1871. Before that, it was hundreds of independent states, each developing its own dialect and vocabulary. Austria and Switzerland went their own way even earlier.

Swiss German (Schweizerdeutsch) is so different from Standard German that Germans often cannot understand it. Austrian German (Osterreichisches Deutsch) is closer but has many unique words, especially for food.

The Goethe B1 exam accepts all standard variants. 'Semmel' is as correct as 'Brötchen.' Understanding regional variants is part of B1 competence.
cream (Swiss German)
Standard in Switzerland. 'Rahmkäse' = cream cheese in Swiss German.

A: In Switzerland they say Rahm.
B: Instead of Sahne?
A: Exactly. Rahm, not Sahne.
B: Interesting! Same product.
Standard in Switzerland. 'Rahmkäse' = cream cheese in Swiss German. In Germany: Sahne. Same product, different countries.
whipped cream (Swiss)
Schlag + Rahm. On Swiss cafe menus where Germans see Schlagsahne.

A: A coffee with whipped cream, please.
B: You are from Switzerland?
A: Yes! In Zürich we say Schlagrahm.
B: Here it is called Schlagsahne.
On a Swiss cafe menu where a German menu says Schlagsahne, you find Schlagrahm. Same product, different tradition.
carrot (northern German)
In Hamburg, you hear Möhre. In Munich: Karotte.

A: Möhren or Karotten?
B: In Hamburg: Möhren!
A: Do they taste different?
B: No, only the name is different.
Verumius learned Karotte first, now hears Möhre in Hamburg. In some regions: 'Gelbe Rübe.' Three words for one vegetable.
jam/preserve (Swiss/formal)
EU regulation says non-citrus jam must be called Konfitüre. Swiss standard.

A: That is Konfitüre, not Marmelade.
B: What is the difference?
A: Technically only citrus = Marmelade.
B: I did not know that!
EU regulation says only citrus preserves can be 'Marmelade.' Everything else is technically 'Konfitüre.' The Swiss just followed the rule from the start.
whipped cream (Austrian)
Schlag + Obers (cream, Austrian). In a Viennese Kaffeehaus: essential.

A: A Melange with whipped cream, please!
B: That sounds Viennese!
A: Yes, in Vienna they say Schlagobers.
B: And in Berlin: Schlagsahne.
In a Viennese Kaffeehaus: 'Einen Melange mit Schlagobers, bitte!' In Berlin, that same order is 'Milchkaffee mit Schlagsahne.' Language geography in action.
Während takes genitive. Reise is feminine. What does 'die' become in genitive?
Kollege is N-Deklination. What ending does it take in accusative?
Verumius met the colleague on the platform.
Switzerland uses Rahm for cream. Whipped = Schlag + Rahm.
DURING the stay. Aufenthalt is masculine. Genitive masculine: des.
During the stay in Hamburg Verumius visited the Elbphilharmonie.
